coredata.dc:title: β-Glucuronidase-triggered reaction for fluorometric and colorimetric dual-mode assay based on the in situ formation of silicon nanoparticles
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yue
Liu, Weiping
Jiang, Xinxin
Liu, Hongmei
Wang, Sikai
Mao, Xiaoqian
Bai, Ruyu
Wen, Yulu
Luo, Xiaojun
Zhang, Guoqi
Zhao, Yan
coredata.dc:description:
Background β-Glucuronidase (GUS) is considered as a promising biomarker for primary cancer. Thus, the reliable detection of GUS has great practical significance in the discovery and diagnosis of cancer. Compared with traditional organic probes, silicon nanoparticles (Si NPs) have emerged as robust optical nanomaterials due to their facile preparation, superior photobleaching resistance and excellent biocompatibility. However, most nanomaterials-based methods only output a single signal which is easily influenced by external factors in complex systems. Hence, developing nanomaterial-based multi-signal optical assays for highly sensitive GUS determination is still urgently desired.
Results In this study, we developed a simple and efficient one-step method for the in situ preparation of yellow color and yellow-green fluorescent Si NPs. This was achieved by combining 3-[2-(2-aminoethylamino) ethylamino] propyl-trimethoxysilane with p-aminophenol (AP) in an aqueous solution. The obtained Si NPs showed yellow-green fluorescence at 535 nm when excited at 380 nm, while also exhibiting an absorption peak at a wavelength of 490 nm. Taking inspiration from the easy synthesis step regulated by AP, which is generated through the hydrolysis of 4-aminophenyl β-D-glucuronide catalyzed by GUS, we constructed a direct fluorometric and colorimetric dual-mode method to measure GUS activity. The developed fluorometric and colorimetric sensing platform showed high sensitivity and accuracy with detection limits for GUS determination as low as 0.0093 and 0.081 U/L, respectively.
Significance This study provides a facile dual-mode fluorometric and colorimetric approach for determination of GUS activity based on novel Si NPs for the first time. This designed sensing approach was successfully employed for the quantification of GUS in human serum samples and screening of GUS inhibitors, indicating the feasibility and potential applications in clinical cancer diagnosis and anti-cancer drug discovery.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Silicon nanoparticles
Dual-mode assay
β-Glucuronidase
Inhibitor screening

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: GUS determination in human serum samples
Synthesis and characterization of Si NPs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: West Light Foundation of the Chinese Academy of Sciences

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by West Light Foundation of the Chinese academy of sciences (to YZ), and the Science and Technology Programs of Sichuan Province of China (No. 2021YFS0060, 2023NSFSC1093).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image ((inset: size distribution histogram), (B) XRD spectrum, (C) FT-IR, and (D) XPS spectrum of Si NPs. High-resolution XPS spectra of (E) N 1s and (F) Si 2p of Si NPs.

(A) UV–vis absorption and fluorescent spectra of Si NPs. Insets are the photographs of Si NPs solution under daylight and under 365 nm UV light. (B) Fluorescence spectra of Si NPs with the excitation wavelength increasing from 350 to 450 nm.

(A) Fluorescence and (C) UV–vis absorption spectra of Si NPs with different concentrations of AP. (B) Fluorescence intensity and (D) absorbance of AEEA toward AP with various concentrations. Insets are the corresponding photographs.

(A) Fluorescence and (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of AEEA (black line), AEEA + GUS (red line), AEEA + APG (blue line), and GUS + APG + AEEA (green line). The amount of each reagent added to the system: 160 μL AEEA, 100 μL APG (1 mM), and 100 μL GUS (50 U/L). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) Fluorescence and (C) UV–vis absorption spectra of the sensing system with different GUS activities (0.01–125 U/L). (B) Fluorescence intensity at 535 nm and (D) absorbance at 490 nm versus various GUS activities. Insets in (A) and (C) are the corresponding photographs under 365 nm UV light and natural light, respectively.

(A) Fluorescence and (C) UV–vis absorption spectra of the GUS assay with different concentrations of baicalin (0–1400 μM). Sigmoidal fitting of the (B) fluorescence intensity and (D) absorbance versus the logarithm of baicalin concentrations. Insets in (A) and (C) are the corresponding photographs under 365 nm UV light and natural light, respectively.
Schematic illustration of the designed approach for GUS activity assay.

head.ce:title: β-Glucuronidase-triggered reaction for fluorometric and colorimetric dual-mode assay based on the in situ formation of silicon nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Science, Xihua University, Chengdu, 610039, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Science, Xihua University, Chengdu, 610039, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Clinical Laboratory, Zigong First People's Hospital, Zigong, 643000, Sichuan, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Clinical Laboratory, Zigong First People's Hospital, Zigong 643000, Sichuan, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Yue Li and Weiping Liu contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: β-Glucuronidase (GUS) is considered as a promising biomarker for primary cancer. Thus, the reliable detection of GUS has great practical significance in the discovery and diagnosis of cancer. Compared with traditional organic probes, silicon nanoparticles (Si NPs) have emerged as robust optical nanomaterials due to their facile preparation, superior photobleaching resistance and excellent biocompatibility. However, most nanomaterials-based methods only output a single signal which is easily influenced by external factors in complex systems. Hence, developing nanomaterial-based multi-signal optical assays for highly sensitive GUS determination is still urgently desired.

In this study, we developed a simple and efficient one-step method for the in situ preparation of yellow color and yellow-green fluorescent Si NPs. This was achieved by combining 3-[2-(2-aminoethylamino) ethylamino] propyl-trimethoxysilane with p-aminophenol (AP) in an aqueous solution. The obtained Si NPs showed yellow-green fluorescence at 535 nm when excited at 380 nm, while also exhibiting an absorption peak at a wavelength of 490 nm. Taking inspiration from the easy synthesis step regulated by AP, which is generated through the hydrolysis of 4-aminophenyl β-D-glucuronide catalyzed by GUS, we constructed a direct fluorometric and colorimetric dual-mode method to measure GUS activity. The developed fluorometric and colorimetric sensing platform showed high sensitivity and accuracy with detection limits for GUS determination as low as 0.0093 and 0.081 U/L, respectively.

This study provides a facile dual-mode fluorometric and colorimetric approach for determination of GUS activity based on novel Si NPs for the first time. This designed sensing approach was successfully employed for the quantification of GUS in human serum samples and screening of GUS inhibitors, indicating the feasibility and potential applications in clinical cancer diagnosis and anti-cancer drug discovery.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: β-Glucuronidase (GUS), a crucial lysosomal glycosidase widely distributed in cellular membranes and extracellular matrices of both normal and malignant tissues [1,2], can catalyze the hydrolysis of glucuronide from glycosaminoglycans, thereby playing a pivotal role in promoting growth, invasion, and metastasis of cancerous cells [3–5]. Abnormal overexpression of GUS is thought to be related to various malignancies including renal carcinoma [6], colon carcinoma [7], prostate cancer [8], liver cancer [9], gastric cancer [10], and pancreatic cancer [11]. Thus, GUS has been taken as an important tumor biomarker. Additionally, many physiological diseases, including urinary tract infection, cholelithiasis, rheumatoid arthritis, renal diseases, and hepatic disorders, are also related to elevated GUS levels in serum [12–15]. Inhibition of GUS activity is essential for treating a variety of cancers. Nevertheless, synthetic drugs like irinotecan, which is a well-known anti-cancer drug, may lead to a series of adverse effects, including diarrhea, vomiting, and nausea [16]. For this reason, seeking effective GUS inhibitors from natural resources without side effects is highly desired for treating cancer and other GUS-related diseases. Hence, it is important to establish a simple and accurate approach for GUS determination and inhibitor screening in early-stage diagnosis and therapy of cancer.

Up to now, varieties of detection methods including colorimetric assays [17,18], phosphorescent assay [19], electrochemical assay [20,21], and fluorometric assay [1,22,23] have been designed for GUS sensing. Among these methods, fluorometric and colorimetric assays stand out owing to their low cost, real-time detecting ability, and operability. Nevertheless, the majority of conventional optical GUS activity assays are based on the variations of color or fluorescence caused by specially designed substrates or products (organic molecules), which may suffer from poor sensitivity and complex synthesis steps. Compared with organic molecule probes, the growing focus on nanomaterials is attributed to their superior photobleaching resistance and excellent biocompatibility [24,25]. Currently, a variety of nanomaterials, such as carbon dots [26,27], metal-organic frameworks [28], and silicon nanoparticles (Si NPs) [29] have been developed for GUS analysis. However, most of the above nanomaterials-based methods only output a single signal which is easily influenced by many external factors in complex systems. Methods with dual-signal or multiple-signal can greatly reduce interferences and the analysis results would be more reliable [30]. What's more, the entire measurement process will become simple and direct if the signal formation depends on in situ produced nanomaterials rather than pre-synthesized nanomaterials. Hence, developing multi-signal optical assays based on GUS-triggered in situ production of nanomaterials for GUS determination with high sensitivity has great significance.

Compared to noble metal nanoclusters, organic fluorescent molecules, and conventional toxic heavy metal based quantum dots, Si NPs have attained wide interest due to their good biocompatibility, superior water dispersibility, low toxicity, and remarkable optical properties, which render them extensively utilized in the biological field [31]. Compared with complicated synthetic procedures required for other nanomaterials, Si NPs can be rapidly prepared under mild conditions by one-step reduction of organosilanes including anilino-methyl-triethoxysilane (AMTS), 3-(2-aminoethylamino)propyl-dimethoxymethylsilane (AEAPMS), 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), and N-(3-triethoxysilylpropyl) ethylenediamine (AEATES) [32]. Nsanzamahoro et al. developed a colorimetric and fluorometric assay for the determination of α-glucosidase activity utilizing Si NPs produced from AEAPMS and hydroquinone [33]. Chen et al. prepared Si NPs by mixing AEATES and p-aminophenol (AP) through a simple one-step synthetic strategy to determine alkaline phosphatase activity and prostate-specific antigen in human serum [34]. The above previous reports verified that in situ determination based on Si NPs possesses significant potential for enzyme-related sensing.

Herein, we developed a simple and accurate dual-mode fluorometric and colorimetric approach to determine GUS activity (Scheme 1 ). We innovatively used 4-aminophenyl β-D-glucuronide (APG) as the GUS substrate, and through the hydrolysis of GUS, AP was released from APG. Based on this, Si NPs owning both fluorometric and colorimetric signals were in situ formed through a simple one-step method applying 3-[2-(2-aminoethylamino) ethylamino] propyl-trimethoxysilane (AEEA) and the product AP for the first time. With the increase of GUS concentration, it was observed that the optical signal of Si NPs gradually increased. The designed dual-mode sensing approach was successfully utilized for GUS assay in human serum samples and inhibitor screening.

Cortex Mori, Granati Pericarpium, Scutellaria baicalensis, Aloe vera, Glycyrrhiza uralensis Fisch., Lonicera japonica Thunb., and Ginkgo Folium were purchased from Beijing Tong Ren Tang Chengdu pharmacy (Chengdu, China). AP, AEEA, APTES, N-[3-(trimethoxysilyl) propyl] ethylenediamine (AEATMS), 3-trimethoxysilylpropan-1-amine (APTMS), (3-methacryloxypropyl) trimethoxysilane (APTMOS), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), 4-nitrophenyl-β-D-glucuronide (PNPG), 4-methylumbelliferyl-β-D-glucuronide (MUG), 4-nitrophenol, 4-methylumbelliferone and GUS were bought from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Isoleucine (Ile), aspartic acid (Asp), lysine (Lys), valine (Val), glutamic acid (Glu), cysteine (Cys), tryptophan (Trp), arginine (Arg), glycine (Gly), human serum albumin (HSA), α-glucosidase (α-Glu), alkaline phosphatase (AKP), thrombin (TB), xanthine oxidase (XOD), trypsin (TRY), amylase (AMS), uric acid (UA), ascorbic acid (AA), and glucose (Glu) were procured from Sigma-Aldrich Chemical Co. (St. Louis, MO, USA). APG was purchased from Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Human serum samples, comprising both healthy and cancer samples, were procured from Zigong First People's Hospital.

Fluorescence spectra were determined with a HORIBA FluoroMax-4 spectrofluorometer (HORIBA, Japan). UV–vis absorption spectra were carried out using a Shimadzu UV-2700 spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra were acquired on a Nicolet iS10 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were performed on a D8 Advance instrument (Bruker, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were collected using an ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken on an FEI Talos F200X electron microscope (FEI, USA).

Si NPs were prepared through blending of AEES and AP at a moderate temperature. In brief, 10.90 mg of AP was completely dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water to produce a transparent solution, followed by adding 160 μL of AEEA. With consecutive stirring, the above-mentioned solution was processed under 60 °C in a water bath for 25 min. Subsequently, the resulting solution was cooled to room temperature and dialyzed using a dialysis bag (1000 Da) for 24 h to eliminate excess reactants. At last, the dialysate Si NPs were preserved at 4 °C for subsequent investigations.

For the detection of GUS activity, 500 μL of PBS (50 mM, pH 7.0) and 100 μL of APG (1 mM) solution were incubated with 100 μL of GUS solution with various activities at 35 °C for 45 min. Then, the resultant solution was then supplemented with 160 μL of AEEA and subjected to incubation at 60 °C for 25 min. Finally, the UV–vis absorption spectra (350–800 nm) and fluorescence emission spectra (λ ex/λ em = 380/535 nm) of the mixture were measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GUS determination in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: Human serum samples were diluted 10-fold with PBS (50 mM, pH 7.0). The diluted human serums (100 μL) were added to the system instead of the standard GUS solution for the determination of GUS activity. Subsequently, the serum samples were spiked with three different levels of GUS solutions (2, 4, and 10 U/L). The recovery (%) was calculated according to the following equation: Recovery (%) = [(A1-A2)/A3] × 100, where A1 is the measured amount of spiked sample, A2 is the measured amount of sample without spiking, and A3 is the theoretical spiked amount. Afterward, the fluorometric and colorimetric assay was carried out according to the steps mentioned in the above section for GUS activity determination.

The feasibility of this screening strategy was verified using baicalin, a potent anti-cancer herbal flavonoid and GUS inhibitor, as an example. The experimental protocol proceeded as follows: baicalin solution with various concentrations (100 μL) was incubated with 100 μL of GUS (100 U/L) at room temperature for 10 min. The mixture was then supplemented with 100 μL of APG (1 mM) and 500 μL of PBS (50 mM, pH 7.0), followed by incubation at 35 °C for 10 min. Subsequently, 160 μL of AEEA was added and the reaction mixture was maintained at a temperature of 60 °C in a water bath for 25 min. Finally, absorbance and fluorescence emission intensity measurements were conducted in the same procedures as the aforementioned experiment.

Moreover, the inhibitory abilities of Cortex Mori, Granati Pericarpium, Scutellaria baicalensis, Aloe vera, Glycyrrhiza uralensis Fisch., Lonicera japonica Thunb., and Ginkgo Folium were further assessed using the proposed strategy. The extraction process was conducted as follows. Firstly, the dried medicinal plant powder (1.0 g) was extracted with 40 mL of 80% methanol for 60 min under reflux. Then, the extraction was filtered and concentrated until dryness. Finally, the residues were dissolved in PBS (50 mM, pH 7.0) for subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of Si NPs
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, AEEA, an organosilane compound with three amine groups, serves as the source of silicon, while AP acts as the reducing agent. We evaluated the effect of different organosilane molecules (AEEA, AEATMS, APTMS, APTES, APTMOS, and TEOS) on the fluorescence intensity of Si NPs when reacting with AP. The results indicated that the Si NPs formed by AEEA displayed the strongest fluorescence intensity (Fig. S1). The process of Si NPs generation was analogous to the one elucidated by Wu et al. [35]. Through introducing organosilane molecule (AEEA) into an aqueous solution that included the reduction reagent (AP), large Si NPs were generated by the hydrolysis of AEEA and the reduction of AP. To attain Si NPs with the most favorable fluorescence performance, the reaction conditions, including temperature (60 °C), reaction time (25 min), and pH value (7.0) were optimized (Fig. S2).

As presented in the TEM image (Fig. 1 A), the prepared Si NPs exhibited a nearly spherical shape with excellent monodispersibility. The size distribution of the Si NPs ranged from 1.37 to 4.43 nm, with an average size of 2.52 nm (inset in Fig. 1A). XRD pattern of the Si NPs (Fig. 1B) showed a single peak (2θ) centered at approximately 23.6°, indicating their amorphous structure.

The surface groups of Si NPs were confirmed by FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 1C). The observed absorption peaks at 3280–3365 and 1600 cm−1 can be ascribed to the stretching vibration of O-H/N-H and bending vibration of N-H, respectively. The characteristic bands that appeared at 2930 and 1470 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibration and bending vibration of the C-H group, respectively. The peak at 1320 cm−1 corresponded to the C-O bond. The intense peaks at 1030-1120 cm−1 pertained to the stretching vibration Si-O-Si, indicating the occurrence of hydrolytic condensation reaction of AEEA during the synthesis process of Si NPs. The band at 1029 cm−1 was ascribed to the Si-C bond. The presence of absorption peaks corresponding to Si-O-Si and Si-C was detected following the combination of AEEA and AP, indicating that neither AEEA nor AP individually had the capability to generate Si NPs in the system. The absorption at 925 cm−1 was related to the stretching vibration of Si-O bond in the Si-O-H group. The absorption bands at 765 and 690 cm−1 were ascribed to the N-H wagging vibration and the secondary amine, respectively. FT-IR data provided evidence for the abundance of –OH and –NH2 groups present on the surface of Si NPs, which contributed to highly hydrophilic and good water dispersibility.

Moreover, the molecular structure and chemical composition of Si NPs were further characterized by XPS. The full survey of XPS analysis exhibited five peaks at 102.5, 153.0, 284.2, 399.1, and 531.2 eV corresponding to Si 2p, Si 2s, C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively (Fig. 1D). The high-resolution spectrum of C 1s presented four major peaks corresponding to C-Si (283.9 eV), CC/C-C (284.5 eV), C-N (285.1 eV), and C-O (285.8 eV), respectively (Fig. S3A). The O 1s spectrum (Fig. S3A) displayed three distinct peaks attributed to Si-O (530.9 eV), Si-O (531.7 eV), and C-O (532.2 eV), respectively (Fig. S3B). The N 1s spectrum indicated the existence of N-Si (398.3 eV), N-C (399.0 eV), and N-O (399.6 eV), respectively (Fig. 1E). The high-resolution spectrum of Si 2p showed three peaks belonging to Si-C (101.2 eV), Si-N (102.3 eV), and Si-O (102.6 eV), respectively (Fig. 1F), in line with the existence of four-valent silicon (Si (4)) [36]. Nevertheless, there were not any distinctive peaks corresponding to zero-valent silicon (Si (0)) (98.4 eV). The above results of XPS were consistent with the FT-IR data.

The optical characteristics of the prepared Si NPs were evaluated through UV–vis absorption, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra. As depicted in Fig. 2A, Si NPs presented three absorption bands at around 380, 490, and 510 nm. The absorption peak at 380 nm was attributed to the n-π* transition of C-O/CN, while the absorption peaks at 490 and 510 nm were related to the surface state transition [37]. The maximum fluorescence excitation and emission wavelength of Si NPs were observed at 380 and 535 nm, respectively. Moreover, under a 365 nm UV lamp, the light-brown solution of Si NPs emitted strong yellow-green fluorescence (inset in Fig. 2 A). As presented in Fig. 2B–a slight red-shift of the emission wavelength was observed as the excitation wavelength ranged from 350 to 450 nm. The excitation-dependent fluorescence property might be due to the oxygen-induced defects on the surface of Si NPs [38]. In addition, the relative fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of Si NPs was measured as 19.8% with quinine sulfate (QY 54%) as a reference (Fig. S4).

The stability of Si NPs was investigated in different conditions. As depicted in Fig. S5A, the fluorescence intensity of Si NPs kept stable after 180 min of light irradiation. Moreover, Fig. S5B showed that Si NPs possessed high pH stability in the wide pH range of 3–11. Besides, as illustrated in Fig. S5C, there was no discernible alteration in the fluorescence intensity of Si NPs even at high concentrations of NaCl (3 M). All the results demonstrated that the prepared Si NPs had good stability, which was beneficial to their biosensing application. The remarkable stability of Si NPs may be attributed to the presence of polar groups, such as hydroxyl and amino groups, on the surface of Si NPs. These functional groups act as a protective coating that effectively inhibits oxidation and enhances the overall stability of the Si NPs [39].

AP, as a reducing agent, can be released from the hydrolysis of APG by GUS for Si NPs preparation. Therefore, the proposed method has the ability to determine GUS activity via GUS-triggered in situ generation of Si NPs. The relationship between the fluorescence and absorption signals of Si NPs and AP concentrations was assessed. As displayed in Fig. 3 A–D, both the fluorescence intensity and absorbance of the AEEA-AP solution gradually enhanced as the AP concentration rose. Meanwhile, the color of the reaction solution turned from colorless to yellow under natural light and yellow-green under 365 nm UV light, respectively (insets of Fig. 3B–D). The AP concentration-controlled optical signals of Si NPs proved the potential for the following determination of GUS activity.

In addition, the feasibility of this GUS assay was verified by fluorescence and UV–vis spectra. Upon addition of GUS-treated APG to the AEEA solution, strong fluorometric and colorimetric signals would be visible (d, Fig. 4 A and B). By contrast, the solutions of AEEA, AEEA-GUS, and AEEA-APG were unable to display fluorescence peaks (a, b, and c, Fig. 4A) or absorption peaks of Si NPs (a, b, and c, Fig. 4B). These findings indicated that AEEA is sensitive only to the GUS-hydrolyzed APG, verifying the possibility of GUS determination.

To obtain excellent sensing performance for GUS activity assay, experimental parameters including substrate (APG) concentration, enzymatic reaction temperature, enzymatic reaction time, the amount of AEEA, and reaction pH were optimized. As shown in Fig. S6A, the APG concentration was investigated and optimized to be 1 mM. The impact of enzymatic reaction temperature from 15 to 50 °C was studied. As presented in Figs. S6B and 35 °C was selected as the optimum temperature for enzymatic reaction. Furthermore, it was found that an enzymatic reaction time of 40 min yielded the highest fluorescence intensity (Fig. S6C). The effect of the amount of AEEA was also investigated. Fig. S6D showed that the fluorescence intensity significantly increased with the increase of AEEA volume in the range of 40–160 μL, then kept almost unchanged when the volume of AEEA exceeded 160 μL. Thus, 160 μL of AEEA was chosen for subsequent experiments. The impact of reaction pH ranging from 4 to 10 on fluorescence intensity was investigated. As illustrated in Fig. S7, the highest fluorescence intensity was observed at pH 7.0, which was selected as the optimal pH value.

Under the optimum experimental parameters, the fluorometric and colorimetric sensing ability of GUS were investigated. As depicted in Fig. 5 , the obtained Si NPs displayed an increase in optical signals that were dependent on GUS activity, accompanied by a distinct color transformation (inset of Fig. 5A–C). The fluorescence emission intensity of Si NPs at 535 nm displayed good linear relationships with the GUS activities within the range of 0.02–2.0 U/L (R2 = 0.9929) and 2.0–100 U/L (R2 = 0.9904) (inset of Fig. 5B). Meanwhile, the absorbance at 490 nm also exhibited a good linear range with the GUS activity within the range of 0.2–20 U/L (R2 = 0.9938) (inset of Fig. 5D). The limits of detection (LOD) of GUS activity for both fluorometric and colorimetric assay were calculated to be 0.0093 and 0.081 U/L, respectively (calculated based on 3σ/K, σ stands for the standard deviation of the 11 replicate measurements of blank samples, and K stands for the slope of linear calibration curve). As presented in Table S1, the proposed method demonstrated a lower LOD and a wider linear range compared to previous reports, indicating its high sensitivity and outstanding analytical capabilities. More importantly, our developed dual-mode sensing platform can enhance the reliability and effectiveness of analysis results, making it appropriate for routine determination of GUS in authentic samples.

To assess the selectivity and anti-interference ability of the proposed approach, the influence of various potential interfering substances such as metal ions (Co2+, Al3+, Na+, Mn4+, K+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Mg2+), amino acids (Lys, Ile, Asp, Cys, Val, Glu, Trp, Arg, Gly), biomolecules (α-Glu, AMS, AKP, TB, XOD, HAS, TRY, AA, UA, Glu) on both fluorescence intensity and absorbance were investigated. As depicted in Fig. S8, the interfering substances exhibited negligible effects on the sensing system, suggesting that the designed sensing method presented satisfactory selectivity and anti-interference ability for the GUS activity assay.

To further evaluate the reliability of the proposed dual-mode assay, we determined GUS content in human serum samples. Normal human serum samples were spiked with three different concentrations (2, 4, 10 U/L) of GUS. As presented in Table S2, the mean recoveries determined by the fluorometric approach were 96.0–103.7%, with relative standard deviations (RSDs) of 1.4–3.6% (intra-day, n = 3) and 2.3–5.3% (inter-day, n = 9), respectively. Similarly, the mean recoveries of the colorimetric method were 96.0%–107.0%, with RSDs of 1.5–4.7% (intra-day, n = 3) and 2.1–5.4% (inter-day, n = 9), respectively. In addition, t-test was performed to compare the GUS content in serum samples of 20 different cancer patients (gastric cancer, right upper lobe tumor, lung cancer, renal cancer, rectal cancer with liver metastasis, and colorectal cancer) determined by our proposed fluorometric and colorimetric approaches. As presented in Table S3, the t-values of measurements were all less than the critical value (2.776, at 95% confidence interval), indicating that the data obtained from the two detection modes exhibited no significant difference. Furthermore, the level of GUS activity in serum samples from cancer patients (Table S3) was obviously higher than that of normal individuals (Table S2), suggesting that GUS activity could be used as a meaningful clinical indicator for cancer diagnosis. In addition, as displayed in Table S4 and Table S5, the results obtained from our proposed colorimetric and fluorometric approaches were highly consistent with those obtained from the standard PNPG-based colorimetric method and the standard MUG-based fluorometric method, respectively. These data demonstrated that the sensing approach was applicable to the detection of GUS activity in complex biological samples with acceptable reliability.

Baicalin, a well-acknowledged GUS inhibitor and potent anti-cancer herbal flavonoid [40], was selected as a representative compound to evaluate the capability of the proposed fluorometric and colorimetric assay in GUS inhibitor screening. As presented in Fig. 6 A and C, a gradual decrease in fluorescence intensity and absorbance occurred as the concentration of baicalin increased within the range of 0–1400 μM. Classical sigmoidal curves were built by plotting the fluorescence and absorption signals versus the logarithm of baicalin concentration. Additionally, the half maximal inhibitory concentrations (IC50) of baicalin for both fluorometric and colorimetric measurements were calculated to be 37.4 and 18.2 μM from the sigmoidal curves, respectively (Fig. 6B–D), which were basically consistent with previously reported studies [29]. These results demonstrated that the designed sensing method was feasible for the screening of GUS inhibitors.

Furthermore, the development of potent and non-toxic inhibitors for therapeutic purposes from natural plants has attracted increasing attention [41]. As illustrated in Fig. S9, the IC50 values of the methanol extracts from Cortex Mori, Granati Pericarpium, Scutellaria baicalensis, Aloe vera, Glycyrrhiza uralensis Fisch., Lonicera japonica Thunb., and Ginkgo Folium were 2.57, 4.65, 1.14, 5.31, 6.98, 4.52 and 3.54 mg/mL, respectively. Hence, this sensing assay could be utilized to screen GUS inhibitors from natural resources.

In conclusion, novel Si NPs owning both fluorometric and colorimetric signals have been fabricated via a one-step process involving the mixing of AP and AEEA solutions under mild conditions for the first time. The obtained Si NPs with a quantum yield of 19.8% exhibited intense yellow-green fluorescence at 535 nm when excited at a wavelength of 380 nm. Furthermore, they demonstrated excellent water dispersibility and favorable optical properties. The fluorescence and absorption signals of produced Si NPs are associated with the concentration of AP which can be released from the hydrolysis of APG by GUS. Therefore, a dual-mode GUS activity measurement was carried out based on the in situ formation of Si NPs. The proposed sensing method showed satisfactory selectivity and sensitivity for the determination of GUS activity for both fluorometric and colorimetric approaches, with LODs of 0.0093 and 0.081 U/L, respectively. In addition, the practicability of the proposed sensing approach was demonstrated through determining GUS activity in real human serum samples and screening for GUS inhibitors from natural plants. This study not only serves as a valuable tool for GUS-related analysis using innovative materials and novel approaches, with the potential to enhance detection performance, but also has broad prospects in the fields of clinical medical diagnosis and pharmaceutical research.

Yue Li: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Weiping Liu: Investigation, Resources. Xinxin Jiang: Data curation, Investigation, Methodology, Validation. Hongmei Liu: Investigation, Validation. Sikai Wang: Investigation, Methodology. Xiaoqian Mao: Resources. Ruyu Bai: Investigation, Validation. Yulu Wen: Investigation, Methodology. Xiaojun Luo: Funding acquisition, Project administration. Guoqi Zhang: Project administration, Supervision. Yan Zhao: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by West Light Foundation of the Chinese academy of sciences (to YZ), and the Science and Technology Programs of Sichuan Province of China (No. 2021YFS0060, 2023NSFSC1093).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2024.342471.
